function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Distinguish precisely among Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts. Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer, which has the highest count, and why?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about the distinction among "Starlink Subscriber," "Service Line," and "supported accounts" in the context of the SpaceX S-1 filing. I need to retrieve the relevant definitions and data from the filing to answer this question precisely.

Let me first parse the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieve the relevant information about these terms.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts from the filing.

arguments.prompt: What is the definition of Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts in {{spacex_s1}}? Include the exact terminology and any glossary definitions.
What are the counts or numbers for Starlink Subscribers, Service Lines, and supported accounts in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 Preamble glossary and key business metrics sections, here are the exact definitions for the requested terms:

### **Starlink Subscriber**
* **Glossary Definition:** “Starlink Subscriber” refers to a unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that does not have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team.
* **Metric Calculation & Scope:** Starlink Subscribers includes both Personal (e.g., Residential and Roam) and Business (e.g., Local Priority and Global Priority) subscription plans, but **does not include** managed enterprise and government customers with contracts in domains including aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities. The company calculates Starlink Subscribers for a period as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period. 

### **Service Line**
* **Glossary Definition:** “Service Line” refers to an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and billed according to Starlink’s service plans and terms of service. 
* **Distinctions & Usage:** The number of Service Lines is distinct from the number of unique devices, account holders, end users, or physical persons. 
  * An individual, household, or business may share a single Service Line among multiple end-users. 
  * Likewise, an individual, household, or business may maintain multiple service lines (e.g., both a Residential Service Line and a separate Roam Service Line, which would be defined as two separate Service Lines and therefore two Starlink Subscribers).

### **supported accounts**
* **Glossary Definition:** “supported accounts” refers to, when used in the context of our X platform and Grok, a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok. 
* **Inclusions:** The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam, or bot accounts if they are active.

Based on the provided text, here are the counts and numbers for the requested metrics:

**1. Starlink Subscribers**
The text and financial tables provide the following subscriber counts:
* **10.3 million** (as of the quarter ended March 31, 2026)
* **5.0 million** (as of the quarter ended March 31, 2025)
* **8.9 million** (for the full year ended December 31, 2025)
* **4.4 million** (for the full year ended December 31, 2024)
* **2.3 million** (for the full year ended December 31, 2023)
*(Note: The text highlights that the subscriber base grew by 105% year-over-year between Q1 2025 and Q1 2026).*

**2. Service Lines**
The document does not provide a separate, distinct number for "Service Lines" because the company explicitly equates the two metrics. 
* **The Count:** The number of Service Lines is identical to the Starlink Subscribers count (**10.3 million** as of March 31, 2026). 
* **The Definition:** The text states, *"We calculate Starlink Subscribers for a period as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period."* It further clarifies that if an individual or business maintains multiple service lines (e.g., a Residential line and a Roam line), they are counted as two separate Service Lines and therefore two separate Starlink Subscribers.

**3. Supported Accounts**
* **Over 1.3 billion** supported accounts were active in the last twelve months ended March 31, 2026.
* *Context:* This metric is not related to Starlink; rather, it refers to the company's integrated AI and social platforms across **Grok and X**. (The text additionally notes this user base includes approximately 550 million Monthly Active Users (MAUs) and 117 million MAUs specifically using Grok's AI features).

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have the key definitions and counts. Let me also retrieve more detail about the distinction between these metrics and any additional context about revenue-generating customers.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about Starlink revenue, managed enterprise and government customers, and how Starlink Subscribers relate to revenue generation in {{spacex_s1}}?
What does the filing say about X platform revenue, monetization, and supported accounts or monthly active users in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided filing excerpts, here is a detailed breakdown of Starlink's revenue generation, the role of managed enterprise and government customers, and the strategic relationship between Starlink Subscribers and revenue.

### 1. Starlink Revenue Generation & Recognition
The Connectivity segment generates revenue through several distinct channels and pricing models:
*   **Consumer Broadband:** Revenue is primarily driven by monthly subscription fees (based on geographic market and download speed) and the one-time sale of hardware (Starlink Kits). 
*   **Enterprise & Government:** Contracts are customized based on customer needs, structured as a combination of subscriptions, data consumption, capacity, or percentage-of-completion. **Enterprise & Government revenue also includes Starlink Mobile service offerings.**
*   **Starlink Mobile:** The company partners with roughly 30 Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) globally, charging them a fixed fee or a per-mobile-user fee, or earning revenue through revenue-sharing arrangements based on the connectivity services included in the MNOs' plans.
*   **Government & Starshield:** Revenue is generated via long-term contracts for public services, disaster response, and **Starshield** (a secure, dedicated satellite network designed specifically for U.S. Government and national security applications).
*   **Revenue Recognition & Deferred Revenue:** Service revenue is recognized over time (ratably or based on data usage), while hardware revenue is recognized upon delivery or installation. Long-term Starshield contracts use the cost-to-cost input method. Deferred revenue balances (which were $12.1 billion at the end of 2025) primarily relate to Space agreements and Connectivity enterprise/government contracts.

### 2. Managed Enterprise and Government Customers
The filing makes a distinct separation between standard subscribers and "managed" enterprise/government accounts:
*   **Exclusion from Core Metrics:** The filing explicitly states that the "Starlink Subscribers" metric **does not include** managed enterprise and government customers. These managed customers operate in specialized domains such as aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities.
*   **Sales Strategy & Retention:** The company utilizes a direct, vertical-specific sales model with dedicated engineering and sales teams to drive fleet-wide conversions (e.g., partnering with leading airlines). This approach has resulted in exceptional retention: since 2023, **no Starlink Enterprise customer contributing more than $750,000 in annual revenue has voluntarily discontinued their service.**
*   **Growth Driver:** Enterprise and government sales are a massive pillar of the company's financial growth. For example, in 2025, enterprise and government revenue increased by $1.41 billion, driven heavily by aviation, maritime, mobility, and government connectivity businesses.

### 3. Starlink Subscribers and Revenue Strategy (Scale vs. ARPU)
The filing outlines a highly specific philosophy regarding how subscriber growth translates to revenue:
*   **Defining the Subscriber Base:** "Starlink Subscribers" counts unique *Service Lines* (e.g., Residential, Roam, Business) rather than individual humans. Because multiple people can share a household line, or one user can hold multiple lines (e.g., Residential and Roam), the actual number of end-users is meaningfully higher than the reported subscriber count (which reached ~10.3 million by Q1 2026). Consumer subscribers represented over 60% of Connectivity segment revenue in 2025.
*   **The ARPU Trade-off:** Massive subscriber volume growth (e.g., 99.9% growth in 2025; 104.7% in Q1 2026) is the primary engine for consumer revenue. However, this hyper-growth is consistently accompanied by a **decline in Starlink Subscriber ARPU** (Average Revenue Per User), which fell 11.2% in 2025 and 22.9% in Q1 2026. 
*   **Strategic Prioritization of Scale:** The filing explicitly notes that the company's strategy **does not prioritize increasing ARPU**. Instead, the focus is on driving sustainable revenue growth and expanding margins through scale, operational efficiencies, and technological advancements. 
*   **Future Outlook:** The company expects ARPU to continue declining over the next few years. This is an accepted and intentional result of expanding globally outside of North America and introducing lower-priced service plans to maximize global market access and total subscriber volume.

Based on the provided excerpts from the S-1 filing, here is a detailed breakdown of what the prospectus says regarding the X platform's revenue, monetization strategies, supported accounts, and Monthly Active Users (MAUs):

### 1. Supported Accounts and Monthly Active Users (MAUs)
The filing outlines significant scale and growth across the integrated X and Grok platforms, while explicitly defining how these metrics are calculated:
*   **Current Metrics (as of March 31, 2026):** The platforms have over **1.3 billion supported accounts** active in the last twelve months, including approximately **550 million MAUs**. The platforms generate approximately **350 million daily posts**.
*   **Growth Trajectory:** This represents an increase from over 1.1 billion supported accounts and approximately 520 million MAUs as of December 31, 2025.
*   **Grok AI Adoption:** Of the total MAUs, approximately **117 million MAUs** actively used Grok’s AI features as of March 31, 2026.
*   **Metric Definitions & Methodologies:** 
    *   **Supported Accounts:** Defined as any human, bot, or similar account that logged into X or Grok. The filing notes this number *may include fake, spam, or bot accounts* if they are active.
    *   **MAUs:** Users who interacted with Grok or X via web or mobile at least once in a 30-day period. Only registered users are included. The company attempts to deduplicate users who access both platforms based on sign-in traffic, though it acknowledges this is an estimate and may not capture all duplication.
*   **Strategic Priority (Revenue over MAU):** The filing explicitly states that while MAUs estimate user base size and engagement, **the company is focused on revenue and operating margin**. The primary objective is driving sustainable revenue growth and profitability, *rather than* growing or maintaining MAU levels.

### 2. Monetization Strategies and Revenue Streams
The company monetizes the X platform through a diversified mix of advertising, consumer subscriptions, enterprise AI, and emerging financial services:

*   **Advertising (Core Channel):** 
    *   X monetizes user activity through high-impact, performance-based advertising inventory (e.g., Promoted Ads, Vertical Video, Takeovers). 
    *   **AI Integration:** Grok is increasingly used to help advertisers with campaign creation, creative optimization, and aligning with trending topics/user intent. 
    *   **New Ad Platform:** In April 2026, the company began rolling out a rebuilt, AI-powered **X Ads Manager** for more precise, dynamic ad delivery and real-time monitoring.
*   **Consumer Subscriptions:** 
    *   The company is focused on converting free users into paying subscribers via **X Premium/Premium+** and **Grok tiers** (SuperGrok, SuperGrok Heavy, SuperGrok Lite).
    *   **Subscriber Base:** As of March 31, 2026, there were approximately **6.3 million active paid subscribers** (4.4 million for X Premium/Premium+ and 1.9 million for SuperGrok tiers).
*   **Enterprise AI & Data Licensing:**
    *   **Enterprise Deployments:** Tailored deployments of Grok (Grok Business and Grok Enterprise) customized for specific workflows and security needs, sold on license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing models.
    *   **Data Licensing:** Developers and enterprises pay to access, search, and analyze X’s real-time and historical intellectual property/data via APIs.
*   **The "Everything App" & New Channels:**
    *   **X Money:** Launched in beta in November 2025, this product aims to expand platform utility by enabling payments, banking, and financial services.
    *   **X Chat:** Released in November 2025, featuring end-to-end encryption with no connection to ad personalization.

### 3. Revenue Performance (2024 vs. 2025)
The MD&A section highlights a shift in the company's revenue composition, showing a recovery in advertising and massive growth in AI/subscription revenues:
*   **2024 Performance:** Total revenue *decreased* by $341 million (11.5%). This was driven by a massive **$595 million drop in advertising revenue** due to the loss of advertising partners on X. However, this was partially offset by a $254 million increase in AI solutions/infrastructure (driven by X subscriptions and data licensing).
*   **2025 Performance:** Total revenue *increased* by $581 million (22.2%). Advertising revenue rebounded with a **$116 million increase** as ad spend recovered. AI solutions and infrastructure revenue surged by **$465 million**, fueled by a $365 million jump in X/Grok subscription revenue and an $88 million increase in data licensing.
*   **Seasonality:** Advertising revenues experience seasonality, typically peaking in Q4 (end-of-year spend) and hitting their lowest point in Q1.

### 4. Risks to User Base and Monetization
The Risk Factors section outlines several vulnerabilities tied to X's user base and revenue models:
*   **Advertiser Volatility:** Advertisers generally lack long-term commitments to X and may reduce or halt spending for reasons outside the company's control.
*   **User Decline & Content Quality:** If users migrate to other platforms, fail to contribute content, or if the platform is plagued by "problematic or offensive" content, monetizable usage and subscription revenues will decline. This could also cause severe brand and reputational harm.
*   **Impact on AI Development:** A decline in the volume or quality of user-generated content on X directly threatens the ongoing development of Grok, which relies on X's real-time data and human discourse for training and "truth-seeking" contextual awareness.
*   **Fierce Competition in Fintech:** The planned public launch of the "Money Product" pits the company against large, established financial and tech companies with significantly greater resources and market presence.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have comprehensive information to answer the question. Let me synthesize the distinctions among the three metrics:

1. **Starlink Subscriber** - A unique Service Line directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity without a direct negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team. Counted as unique Service Lines at end of period. ~10.3 million as of Q1 2026.

2. **Service Line** - An individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal. One person/entity can have multiple Service Lines. The Starlink Subscriber count equals the number of unique Service Lines (for non-managed customers).

3. **Supported Accounts** - Any human, bot, or similar account that logged into X or Grok. May include fake, spam, or bot accounts. Over 1.3 billion as of Q1 2026.

Now to answer the specific questions:
- Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer? Starlink Subscriber - it represents a billed subscription line directly generating recurring revenue.
- Which has the highest count? Supported accounts at 1.3 billion.
- Why? Because supported accounts include all logged-in accounts on X/Grok (including bots, spam, free users), while Starlink Subscribers are paying broadband customers and Service Lines are the underlying technical units of service.

Let me also note the nuance: Service Lines and Starlink Subscribers are essentially the same count (10.3M) for the consumer segment, but Service Lines is the broader technical concept that also applies to managed enterprise/government customers (who are excluded from the Starlink Subscriber count). So total Service Lines across all of Starlink would actually be higher than the Starlink Subscriber count.

Let me retrieve more information to confirm whether total Service Lines (including enterprise/government) is disclosed.

arguments.prompt: What is the total number of Service Lines including enterprise and government customers in {{spacex_s1}}? Is there a separate count for total Service Lines vs Starlink Subscribers?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, **the total number of Service Lines including enterprise and government customers is not disclosed**, nor is there a **separate count provided for total Service Lines versus Starlink Subscribers**. 

Here is what the text explicitly clarifies regarding these metrics:

*   **Definition of Starlink Subscribers:** The company defines a "Starlink Subscriber" as a unique Service Line assigned to an account that **does not** have a direct, negotiated agreement with the Starlink sales team. 
*   **Exclusion of Enterprise/Government:** The text explicitly states that the "Starlink Subscribers" metric **does not include** managed enterprise and government customers (e.g., aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities). 
*   **Reported Count:** The only Service Line count provided is for "Starlink Subscribers" (the subset excluding enterprise/government), which totaled approximately **10.3 million** as of March 31, 2026. 

While the text establishes that "Starlink Subscribers" is essentially a count of a specific subset of Service Lines, it never provides the numerical count for the excluded enterprise and government Service Lines, making it impossible to determine the combined total or the separate count for those specific segments from this document.

steps.message: Now I have all the information needed to provide a precise, comprehensive answer.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me compose a comprehensive answer.

Key distinctions:
1. **Starlink Subscriber** = a subset of Service Lines (those without direct negotiated agreements). ~10.3M as of Q1 2026. Most analogous to a revenue-generating customer because each represents a billed subscription.
2. **Service Line** = the fundamental technical unit of Starlink service. One account can have multiple Service Lines. The Starlink Subscriber metric is calculated as the count of unique Service Lines for non-managed accounts. Total Service Lines (including enterprise/government) is not disclosed but would be higher.
3. **Supported Accounts** = any account (human, bot, spam) that logged into X or Grok. ~1.3 billion. Highest count by far, but includes non-revenue-generating and non-human accounts.

Most analogous to revenue-generating customer: Starlink Subscriber (each is a paying subscription line)
Highest count: Supported accounts (1.3 billion vs. 10.3 million)

arguments.final_result:
## Distinguishing Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and Supported Accounts

Based on the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026, these three metrics are defined in the filing's glossary and key business metrics sections as follows:

---

### 1. Service Line (The Fundamental Technical Unit)

**Definition:** "An individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service."

**Key characteristics:**
- It is the atomic unit of Starlink service delivery.
- It is explicitly **not** equivalent to a unique device, account holder, end user, or physical person.
- A single individual, household, or business may hold **multiple** Service Lines (e.g., one Residential line and one Roam line = two Service Lines).
- Conversely, multiple end-users may **share** a single Service Line (e.g., a household).
- Service Lines exist across **all** customer categories—consumer, enterprise, and government.

---

### 2. Starlink Subscriber (A Commercially Defined Subset of Service Lines)

**Definition:** "A unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that **does not have a direct, negotiated agreement** with the Starlink sales team."

**Key characteristics:**
- Calculated as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period.
- **Includes** Personal subscriptions (Residential, Roam) and Business subscriptions (Local Priority, Global Priority).
- **Explicitly excludes** managed enterprise and government customers with negotiated contracts in aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities.
- **Count:** ~10.3 million as of March 31, 2026 (up from ~5.0 million as of March 31, 2025—a 105% YoY increase).

Because Starlink Subscribers are defined as a subset of Service Lines, the **total number of Service Lines across all of Starlink (including enterprise/government) is necessarily higher** than the Starlink Subscriber count, though the filing does not disclose the total.

---

### 3. Supported Accounts (An X/Grok Platform Engagement Metric)

**Definition:** "A human, bot, or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok."

**Key characteristics:**
- Applies exclusively to the **X platform and Grok AI**—it has no connection to Starlink.
- **May include fake, spam, or bot accounts** if they are active.
- **Count:** Over **1.3 billion** supported accounts active in the last twelve months ended March 31, 2026 (up from ~1.1 billion as of December 31, 2025).
- The filing separately reports ~550 million Monthly Active Users (MAUs) and ~117 million Grok AI MAUs, both of which are subsets of supported accounts.
- The filing explicitly acknowledges that the company is "focused on revenue and operating margin" rather than growing or maintaining MAU levels.

---

## Comparative Analysis

| Metric | Domain | Count (Q1 2026) | Includes Non-Paying? | Includes Non-Human? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Service Line** | Starlink (all segments) | Not fully disclosed (>10.3M) | No—each is a billed service instance | No |
| **Starlink Subscriber** | Starlink (consumer only) | ~10.3 million | No—each is a paying subscription | No |
| **Supported Accounts** | X / Grok platform | ~1.3 billion | Yes—vast majority are free users | Yes—bots and spam included |

---

## Which Is Most Analogous to a Revenue-Generating Customer?

**Starlink Subscriber** is the most analogous to a revenue-generating customer. Each Starlink Subscriber represents a unique, actively billed subscription line generating recurring monthly service revenue (and typically an upfront hardware sale). The filing ties this metric directly to consumer revenue, noting that consumer subscribers represented over 60% of Connectivity segment revenue in 2025, and tracks Starlink Subscriber ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) as a key financial metric.

A **Service Line** is the closest conceptual equivalent but is a technical/operational unit rather than a commercial one. Importantly, the Starlink Subscriber metric is *derived from* Service Lines—it is literally defined as a count of a specific subset of them.

**Supported Accounts** are the least analogous to revenue-generating customers. The vast majority of the 1.3 billion supported accounts are free, non-paying users (and potentially bots/spam). Only ~6.3 million active paid subscribers (X Premium/Premium+ and SuperGrok tiers) directly generate subscription revenue on the X platform—a tiny fraction of the total.

---

## Which Has the Highest Count?

**Supported accounts** have by far the highest count at **~1.3 billion**, exceeding Starlink Subscribers (~10.3 million) by roughly two orders of magnitude (approximately 126x).

**Why:** Supported accounts measure any login event on a global social media and AI platform over a twelve-month window, with no quality filter (bots, spam, and duplicate accounts are included). Starlink Subscribers, by contrast, measure only paying broadband subscriptions on a satellite network with finite capacity and hardware requirements, and explicitly exclude the enterprise/government segment. The fundamental difference is between an open, free-to-use digital platform with minimal friction to account creation and a paid hardware-dependent satellite internet service.

---

**Sources:**

  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Distinguishing Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and Supported Accounts

Based on the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026, these three metrics are defined in the filing's glossary and key business metrics sections as follows:

---

### 1. Service Line (The Fundamental Technical Unit)

**Definition:** "An individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal or group of terminals, and billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service."

**Key characteristics:**
- It is the atomic unit of Starlink service delivery.
- It is explicitly **not** equivalent to a unique device, account holder, end user, or physical person.
- A single individual, household, or business may hold **multiple** Service Lines (e.g., one Residential line and one Roam line = two Service Lines).
- Conversely, multiple end-users may **share** a single Service Line (e.g., a household).
- Service Lines exist across **all** customer categories—consumer, enterprise, and government.

---

### 2. Starlink Subscriber (A Commercially Defined Subset of Service Lines)

**Definition:** "A unique Service Line that is directly assigned to a Starlink.com account registered to a person or entity that **does not have a direct, negotiated agreement** with the Starlink sales team."

**Key characteristics:**
- Calculated as the number of unique Service Lines at the end of the period.
- **Includes** Personal subscriptions (Residential, Roam) and Business subscriptions (Local Priority, Global Priority).
- **Explicitly excludes** managed enterprise and government customers with negotiated contracts in aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities.
- **Count:** ~10.3 million as of March 31, 2026 (up from ~5.0 million as of March 31, 2025—a 105% YoY increase).

Because Starlink Subscribers are defined as a subset of Service Lines, the **total number of Service Lines across all of Starlink (including enterprise/government) is necessarily higher** than the Starlink Subscriber count, though the filing does not disclose the total.

---

### 3. Supported Accounts (An X/Grok Platform Engagement Metric)

**Definition:** "A human, bot, or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok."

**Key characteristics:**
- Applies exclusively to the **X platform and Grok AI**—it has no connection to Starlink.
- **May include fake, spam, or bot accounts** if they are active.
- **Count:** Over **1.3 billion** supported accounts active in the last twelve months ended March 31, 2026 (up from ~1.1 billion as of December 31, 2025).
- The filing separately reports ~550 million Monthly Active Users (MAUs) and ~117 million Grok AI MAUs, both of which are subsets of supported accounts.
- The filing explicitly acknowledges that the company is "focused on revenue and operating margin" rather than growing or maintaining MAU levels.

---

## Comparative Analysis

| Metric | Domain | Count (Q1 2026) | Includes Non-Paying? | Includes Non-Human? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Service Line** | Starlink (all segments) | Not fully disclosed (>10.3M) | No—each is a billed service instance | No |
| **Starlink Subscriber** | Starlink (consumer only) | ~10.3 million | No—each is a paying subscription | No |
| **Supported Accounts** | X / Grok platform | ~1.3 billion | Yes—vast majority are free users | Yes—bots and spam included |

---

## Which Is Most Analogous to a Revenue-Generating Customer?

**Starlink Subscriber** is the most analogous to a revenue-generating customer. Each Starlink Subscriber represents a unique, actively billed subscription line generating recurring monthly service revenue (and typically an upfront hardware sale). The filing ties this metric directly to consumer revenue, noting that consumer subscribers represented over 60% of Connectivity segment revenue in 2025, and tracks Starlink Subscriber ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) as a key financial metric.

A **Service Line** is the closest conceptual equivalent but is a technical/operational unit rather than a commercial one. Importantly, the Starlink Subscriber metric is *derived from* Service Lines—it is literally defined as a count of a specific subset of them.

**Supported Accounts** are the least analogous to revenue-generating customers. The vast majority of the 1.3 billion supported accounts are free, non-paying users (and potentially bots/spam). Only ~6.3 million active paid subscribers (X Premium/Premium+ and SuperGrok tiers) directly generate subscription revenue on the X platform—a tiny fraction of the total.

---

## Which Has the Highest Count?

**Supported accounts** have by far the highest count at **~1.3 billion**, exceeding Starlink Subscribers (~10.3 million) by roughly two orders of magnitude (approximately 126x).

**Why:** Supported accounts measure any login event on a global social media and AI platform over a twelve-month window, with no quality filter (bots, spam, and duplicate accounts are included). Starlink Subscribers, by contrast, measure only paying broadband subscriptions on a satellite network with finite capacity and hardware requirements, and explicitly exclude the enterprise/government segment. The fundamental difference is between an open, free-to-use digital platform with minimal friction to account creation and a paid hardware-dependent satellite internet service.
